runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 3 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Eissa & Liebman (1996) as a recall anchor on labor-supply responses to tax changes
Saez, Slemrod & Giertz (2012) as a recall anchor on taxable-income/labor-supply elasticity magnitudes
Keane (2011) as a recall anchor on labor-supply elasticity survey evidence
runs.reasoningSummary: My memory is that primary earners, especially prime-age workers, tend to have modest intensive-margin substitution elasticities, generally well below those often used for secondary earners. For a lower-middle earnings group like decile 3 in a U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation, I center on a small positive elasticity around 0.15, with substantial uncertainty but unlikely to be near zero or above about 0.4.
